mnemonics.title: THRASHING Causes
mnemonics.content: THRASHING = TOO LITTLE MEMORY CAUSES EXCESSIVE PAGE FAULTS

mnemonics.title: Thrashing Control Techniques
mnemonics.content: CONTROL THRASHING WITH: WORKING SET + PAGE FAULT FREQUENCY + SUSPENSION + PREPAGING

acronyms.expansion: Page Fault Frequency - measures how often a process needs pages not in memory
acronyms.usage: Used to decide when to allocate more frames or suspend processes

acronyms.expansion: Working Set - pages actively used by a process in recent time
acronyms.usage: If WS > allocated frames, process will thrash

acronyms.expansion: Central Processing Unit - the main processor
acronyms.usage: During thrashing, CPU sits idle while pages swap

acronyms.expansion: Degree of Multiprogramming - number of processes in memory
acronyms.usage: Too high DOP causes thrashing, optimal DOP maximizes CPU utilization

visualTips.visualization: Draw a bell curve: X-axis = Degree of Multiprogramming, Y-axis = CPU Utilization. Rise to peak, then sharp drop = thrashing zone. Label the peak as 'Optimal Point'. Everything after = THRASHING.

Imagine a window sliding over time - the working set is whatever pages are inside the window. If window moves and pages fall out, they're no longer in working set. Need enough frames to hold all current working set pages.
Think of a circle: Page Fault → Load Page from Disk → Process Runs Briefly → Another Page Fault → Repeat. This circle = thrashing cycle. Break it by adding more frames or reducing active processes.

analogies.analogy: Thrashing is like a librarian who keeps fetching books from the basement for readers, but each reader needs different books constantly. The librarian spends all time in the elevator (I/O) and no time actually giving books to readers (CPU work). Adding more readers (processes) makes it worse.

Working set is like your desk - you keep the books and papers you're currently using within arm's reach. If someone gives you a tiny desk (few frames), you constantly have to fetch papers from storage (page faults). A bigger desk (more frames) means fewer trips to storage.

Like a teacher monitoring student performance. If a student (process) asks too many questions (page faults), the teacher gives them more attention (frames). If they ask too few, the teacher redirects resources elsewhere.
